definition: A reaction that releases energy to the surroundings. Aerobic respiration is exothermic — it releases energy as heat.
A reaction that absorbs energy from the surroundings. Photosynthesis is endothermic — it uses light energy.
The process of breaking down glucose using oxygen to release energy. Takes place in the mitochondria. Word equation: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water.
Respiration without oxygen. In animals it produces lactic acid; in yeast it produces ethanol and carbon dioxide. Releases less energy than aerobic respiration.
The process by which green plants make glucose using light energy, carbon dioxide, and water. Takes place in the chloroplasts. Word equation: carbon dioxide + water → glucose + oxygen.
Organelles found in cells where aerobic respiration takes place and energy is released. Singular: mitochondrion.
Organelles found in plant cells that contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis.
The green pigment found in chloroplasts that absorbs light energy for use in photosynthesis.
The net movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration, down a concentration gradient. Requires no energy (passive).
The net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a selectively permeable membrane.
The movement of substances against a concentration gradient (from low to high concentration), using energy released from respiration.
The difference in concentration of a substance between two regions. Particles move down the concentration gradient (from high to low) during diffusion.
A membrane that allows some molecules to pass through but not others. For example, the cell membrane allows water through but not large molecules.
A biological catalyst that speeds up chemical reactions in living organisms. Enzymes are proteins and work best at a specific optimum temperature and pH.
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up itself.
The permanent change in shape of an enzyme's active site caused by high temperature or extreme pH, so it can no longer bind to its substrate.
The specific region on an enzyme where the substrate binds. The shape of the active site is complementary to the shape of the substrate (lock and key model).
The molecule that an enzyme acts on and binds to at its active site.
The temperature or pH at which an enzyme works fastest (maximum rate of reaction).
Cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells. Used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.
Cell division that produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes). Involves two divisions and results in genetic variation.
A cell containing only one set of chromosomes (half the normal number). Gametes (sperm and egg cells) are haploid.
Sex cells — sperm in males, egg cells in females. They are haploid and fuse during fertilisation to form a diploid zygote.
The fusion of a male gamete (sperm) and a female gamete (egg cell) to form a zygote.
The cell formed when a sperm and egg cell fuse during fertilisation. It is diploid and develops into an embryo.
Different versions of the same gene. For example, the gene for eye colour has alleles for blue and brown.
An allele that is always expressed in the phenotype, even when only one copy is present.
An allele that is only expressed in the phenotype when two copies are present (homozygous recessive).
The genetic make-up of an organism — the alleles it carries (e.g. BB, Bb, bb).
The observable characteristics of an organism, produced by the interaction of genotype and environment.
Having two identical alleles for a gene (e.g. BB or bb).
Having two different alleles for a gene (e.g. Bb).
A random change in the sequence of bases in DNA. Mutations can alter the protein produced by a gene and may be inherited.
The process by which individuals with advantageous characteristics are more likely to survive, reproduce, and pass on their alleles to offspring.
The maintenance of a constant internal environment in the body, such as body temperature, blood glucose concentration, and water content.
The loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata. It drives the transpiration stream, pulling water up through the xylem.
Tiny pores in the surface of leaves that allow gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ out) and water vapour to escape. Singular: stoma.
Plant tissue that transports water and dissolved minerals from the roots to the leaves. Made of dead, hollow cells arranged in tubes.
Plant tissue that transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) from the leaves to other parts of the plant.
A factor that limits the rate of a process when it is in short supply. For photosynthesis, limiting factors include light intensity, CO₂ concentration, and temperature.
The position of an organism in a food chain. Producers are at trophic level 1, primary consumers at level 2, and so on.
The variety of different species in an ecosystem. High biodiversity generally indicates a healthy, stable ecosystem.
All the living organisms in an area together with the non-living (abiotic) factors of the environment.
The breakdown of dead organic matter by microorganisms (mainly bacteria and fungi), releasing nutrients back into the soil.
The process where excess nitrates (from fertiliser run-off) cause algal blooms in water, blocking light and leading to death of aquatic organisms.
Medicines that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria. They do not work against viruses.
The ability of bacteria to survive exposure to antibiotics, due to mutations. Resistant strains are not killed and can multiply and spread.
Introducing a weakened or dead pathogen (or its antigens) into the body to stimulate an immune response and the production of memory cells, providing immunity.
Proteins produced by lymphocytes in response to antigens. Each antibody is specific to a particular antigen and helps destroy the pathogen.
Proteins on the surface of pathogens (or other foreign cells) that trigger an immune response.
A hormone produced by the pancreas that lowers blood glucose concentration by causing cells to take up glucose and the liver to store it as glycogen.
A hormone produced by the pancreas that raises blood glucose concentration by causing the liver to break down glycogen into glucose.
The storage form of glucose in animals. Stored in the liver and muscles, it can be broken down to release glucose when needed.
A small circular piece of DNA found in bacterial cells, separate from the main chromosome. Used in genetic engineering to transfer genes between organisms.
Deoxyribonucleic acid — the molecule that carries genetic information in all living organisms. Made up of two strands in a double helix.
A thread-like structure in the nucleus made of DNA, carrying genes. Human body cells have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs).
A section of DNA that codes for a specific protein. Genes are found on chromosomes.
The process by which cells build proteins using the instructions coded in DNA, carried out in ribosomes via transcription and translation.
The net movement of water molecules from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a selectively permeable membrane. Passive process requiring no energy.
A plant cell that has absorbed water by osmosis and whose cell wall is pressing outwards, making the cell firm. Turgidity supports plant tissues.
When a plant cell loses water by osmosis and the cell membrane pulls away from the cell wall. Occurs in concentrated (low water potential) solutions.
The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. It binds with oxygen in the lungs to form oxyhaemoglobin and releases it in body tissues.
The waste product of anaerobic respiration in animal muscle cells. Build-up causes muscle fatigue. It is oxidised when oxygen becomes available again (oxygen debt).
A waste product of anaerobic respiration in yeast (alongside carbon dioxide). Used in fermentation to produce alcoholic drinks and biofuels.
Anaerobic respiration carried out by yeast, producing ethanol and carbon dioxide. Used in baking and brewing.
A junction between two neurones. A nerve impulse is transmitted across the synapse by chemical neurotransmitters.
A rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought. Controlled by the spinal cord via a reflex arc.
A chemical messenger produced by glands, carried in the bloodstream to target organs where it produces an effect.
Antidiuretic hormone — produced by the pituitary gland. It controls water reabsorption in the kidneys, reducing the volume of urine produced when the body is dehydrated.
The process where humans breed plants or animals with desirable characteristics over many generations to improve a species.
The process of cutting a gene from one organism and inserting it into another organism's DNA to give it a desired characteristic (e.g. inserting the human insulin gene into bacteria).
